next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

The window above Junie's kitchen stood open on a slice of pear-colored morning. By early autumn the damp had stopped trying to smother the city and settled for living in its pockets, and the air that came through smelled of river mud gone cool at last, yeast, old boards, and the onion grease already waking below. Silas lay for a moment with one arm over his eyes and felt the ache in his right wrist before he felt anything else. Cold always found the old sprain first. That was one of the small jokes the body kept for itself.

Inez was already up. She stood in her shift and petticoat with one stocking on, tying her dark hair into the work scarf she favored when there was no reason to impress anyone and no point pretending there might be. Her oilskin ledger sat on the chair beside his rolled brushes. His tobacco tin rested on top of it as if the two objects had been born to share a surface. Her boots were under the bed with his, toes touching.

"You awake," she said, not looking around, "or are you planning to bill the morning for your thoughts?"

"Depends who the client is."

"The south ferry line. They pay in bad coffee and complaints."

"Then I'm fully alert."

That got him half a smile. She bent to pull on the second stocking, and the crescent burn scar inside her right wrist flashed pale against her skin before the scarf fell over it. He had learned the scar the way a man learned a favorite bend in a stair rail, by use rather than announcement. The knowledge sat deeper in him than half the names he had once sold for money.

"Captain Lusk wants the new board...
open_hooks_to_carry: Let Gideon's offer be genuinely tempting so refusal costs something real.
Make the contract's destruction materially and symbolically decisive.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ctacle they could purchase later, had to admit what their own eyes were doing to them.

No one stopped arguing as the colors climbed their sleeves and cheeks.

"Potfast and pan-green are close enough," one man insisted.

"Close enough burns babies," a woman answered.

"Dock-green on fog days, rope-light on clear."

"Stair-blue's for stone. Step-blue's for iron."

"Bridelight if you want kissing, you liar."

"Celadon Crown if you've got more money than sense."

The names flew, crossed, collided, and held in separate pockets wherever enough mouths pressed them into use. Bellglass looked diseased and magnificent, like a civic bruise finally showing all the colors that had been gathering under the skin for months. Silas stood in the middle of it with paste drying on his fingers and his wrist throbbing under the old apron tie and understood, with a clarity that felt almost indecent, that he would never again be able to pretend authorship was the same as use.

By full dark the bridge carried six colors and more arguments than a council chamber. People kept stopping midspan to point out which rail had gone step-blue and which lamp brace held wake-blue, until carts stalled and a pair of clerks had to duck between axle and crowd. Even the city inspector, ribbon askew now, stood staring at a gold band cut clean through by cold blue. No one crossing Bellglass could honestly call the moss one thing anymore.

Silas stayed until Damaris's boys had pasted the last blessing bills in wet curls along the south approach, then went back to Ferrymouth with the bridge still arguing in his skull.
state_deltas_required: After the public break, Silas must choose between restored personal success and a diminished but honest future, while Inez refuses sentimental shortcuts.
Silas destroys the contract and forfeits proprietary fame; he and Inez agree to a conditional partnership built on shared work and no sole credit.
